item.title: Does AI want to be free?
item.description: Plus, global imbalances and global crises

item.title: Hermès bumps up against luxury’s scarcity paradox
item.description: There is a tricky balance to strike between exclusivity and scale

item.title: Deterrence costs and we will all have to pay
item.description: There is a need to stop wishful thinking on defence expenditure

item.title: Pensions drawdown: can the 4 Per Cent Rule survive stagflation?
item.description: Ensuring a fund lasts at least 30 years is trickier with asset values falling and prices soaring

item.title: The NHS should keep Palantir for patients’ sake
item.description: The US technology company is opposed by some employees but its software is getting results

item.title: China to drive a weight-loss drug price war
item.description: The active ingredient semaglutide is starting to lose patent protection and China can produce GLP-1 related treatments at scale

item.title: ‘Megadeals’ defy market fears and volatile geopolitics
item.description: Boards should heed the lessons of the last record surge in big M&A in 2015

item.title: Amazon satellite deal takes the battle of the billionaires into orbit
item.description: Bezos and Musk compete for extraterrestrial dominance

item.title: United plus American won’t make US airlines great again
item.description: Tie-up might create a genuine US flag carrier, but there is not much scope to cut prices for passengers

item.title: Magyar is up against the clock in Hungary
item.description: Brussels and Budapest have a rare and fleeting chance to improve Europe’s defence and correct the bloc’s dysfunction

item.title: Iran is America’s Suez crisis — and just as ridiculous
item.description: The parallels with Britain’s disastrous Middle East adventure in the 1950s are impossible to resist

item.title: I’m getting divorced. Should I settle out of court?
item.description: I would like to know the benefits and drawbacks of a financial dispute resolution

item.title: America goes from roommates to boommates
item.description: Baby boomers are now the fastest-growing part of the US room rental market

item.title: The weird resilience of the EU
item.description: A decade into the age of nationalism, the idea of quitting the club is still remarkably taboo

item.title: Defence spending won’t solve Europe’s growth problem
item.description: Plus, what does a healthy labour market look like when there is no job growth?

item.title: The CEO chatbot era is coming
item.description: Many executives will be following Meta’s plans for an AI version of Mark Zuckerberg with interest

item.title: The mix of chaotic politics and a resilient economy can’t last
item.description: The world’s economy has managed to withstand a great deal of chaos, but new IMF forecasts show that things could get worse

item.title: Oil shortages are coming, and with them some difficult questions
item.description: It may not be the early sufferers who experience the most pain if the Strait of Hormuz stays closed for a prolonged period

item.title: Armadillo inspires middle powers in a challenging world
item.description: Would-be architects of a future global order should take heed

item.title: Betting on risk changes the world
item.description: Prediction markets are the latest example of a disruptive new industry smacking into problems incumbents have long understood

item.title: Musk is officially free to roam in Texas
item.description: Delaware ruling draws a line under Tesla’s corporate domicile in the Lone Star State

item.title: America’s tightening cost of living squeeze
item.description: The Iran war has intensified US households’ affordability struggles

item.title: Ad giant Publicis is less ‘Mad Men’, more ‘The IT Crowd’
item.description: Investors are not buying French group’s AI-fuelled growth story

item.title: The US is at risk of an oil shock too
item.description: In a global market for crude, Asian buyers are vacuuming up supplies, raising prices for Europe and America

item.title: As Trump scares off US scientists, China is racing ahead
item.description: From mapping the seabed to AI innovation, Beijing is becoming the world’s scientific superpower
